I Built Raft Consensus From Scratch in Elixir. Here's What the Paper Doesn't Tell You.

PublishedMarch 5, 2026
•
21 min read

Prakash Dakarapu

On this page

The State Machine: Why Elixir Made the Paper DisappearLeader Election: Where Ordering Broke MeThe Crash Before Persist ProblemWho Deserves Your Vote?The No-Op After WinningLog Replication: Where I Spent Most of My Debugging TimeThree Cases When Entries ArriveWhen Is Something Actually Committed?Snapshotting: The Part Everyone UnderestimatesMembership Changes: Where the Paper Undersells the DangerWhy You Can't Just Switch ConfigsJoint Consensus: Requiring Both Sides to AgreeThe Edge Cases That Surprised MeTwo Optimizations That Aren't OptionalPre-Vote: Protecting a Healthy Cluster from a Recovering NodeFast Log BacktrackingArchitectureWhat I'd Tell Someone Building This

I'd read the Raft paper twice. I understood it the way you understand chess rules without having played a real game — I could explain the mechanics, but I'd never felt the weight of a decision where the wrong move corrupts a cluster.

So I sat down and implemented the full thing: leader election, log replication, snapshotting, joint consensus membership changes, and a TCP transport layer. About 1,200 lines of Elixir, a passing test suite, and a long list of assumptions I had to unlearn.

This post is about the unlearning.

Source code on GitHub

The State Machine: Why Elixir Made the Paper Disappear

A Raft node is always in one of four roles: follower, pre_candidate, candidate, or leader. The same incoming message demands completely different handling depending on which role the node currently occupies.

The obvious implementation is a giant case role do that branches for every message type. With 10 message types and 4 roles, that's 40 branches tangled together in one function — and every bug lives somewhere in the tangle.

Erlang/OTP has a better model built into the runtime: gen_statem. Instead of one function with a role variable, you write a separate function per role. The runtime dispatches incoming messages to the right one automatically. You never write dispatch logic.

My module has functions literally named follower, candidate, and leader:

# A follower receives a client command — not my job, redirect.
def follower({:call, from}, {:command, _cmd}, data) do
  {:keep_state, data, [{:reply, from, {:error, {:redirect, data.leader_id}}}]}
end

# A leader receives the same command — actually process it.
def leader({:call, from}, {:command, cmd}, data) do
  handle_client_command(from, cmd, data)
end

# A follower's election timer fires — no heartbeat arrived.
# The leader is probably dead. Start an election.
def follower(:state_timeout, :election_timeout, data) do
  start_pre_vote(data, :follower)
end

# A leader's heartbeat timer fires — remind followers you're alive.
def leader(:timeout, :heartbeat, data) do
  send_heartbeats(data)
end

When a candidate wins an election, it returns {:next_state, :leader, data} and all future messages route to leader/3. When a leader sees a higher term and steps down, {:next_state, :follower, data}. Zero boilerplate.

The other thing gen_statem handles: state timeouts. When a follower starts, I set a randomized timer (150–300ms). If it fires, the node starts an election. If a heartbeat arrives first and the node resets to follower with a fresh timeout, the runtime automatically cancels the old timer. No cleanup code, no stale timers triggering unnecessary elections.

The Raft paper describes rules like: "If election timeout elapses without receiving AppendEntries: convert to candidate." In gen_statem, that rule is one function clause. The paper maps to code almost 1:1.

Almost.

Leader Election: Where Ordering Broke Me

I assumed election would be the straightforward part. Increment a counter, ask for votes, count them. The logic is simple. The ordering nearly cost me correctness.

The Crash Before Persist Problem

When a node starts an election, it does four things:

  1. Increment its term

  2. Vote for itself

  3. Persist both to disk

  4. Send RequestVote RPCs to peers

The order of steps 1–3 matters more than you'd think. Here's the scenario that breaks if you get it wrong:

Node A increments its term to 5 and sends RequestVote. Node B votes for A. A crashes before persisting its own vote. A restarts, has no memory of voting, and grants a vote to Node C — who also started an election in term 5. Now both A and C believe they have a majority. Two leaders in one term.

There's no way around this: write to disk before sending any RPCs.

defp start_election(data, _from_role) do
  new_term = data.current_term + 1

  # Persist FIRST — no RPCs until this is durable.
  :ok = Persistence.save_term(data.node_id, new_term)
  :ok = Persistence.save_vote(data.node_id, data.node_id)

  data = %{data |
    current_term: new_term,
    voted_for: data.node_id,
    votes_received: MapSet.new([data.node_id])
  }

  rpc = %RequestVote{
    term: new_term,
    candidate_id: data.node_id,
    last_log_index: Log.last_index(data.node_id),
    last_log_term:  Log.last_term(data.node_id)
  }

  for peer <- Cluster.peers(data.cluster, data.node_id) do
    RPC.send_rpc(data.node_id, peer, rpc)
  end

  if Cluster.has_majority?(MapSet.size(data.votes_received), Cluster.size(data.cluster)) do
    become_leader(data)
  else
    {:next_state, :candidate, data,
     [{:state_timeout, election_timeout(), :election_timeout}]}
  end
end

For persistence, I used Erlang's DETS — a disk-backed key-value store built into the runtime. Zero external dependencies, but one critical detail: calling :dets.sync/1 after every write. Without it, data sits in an OS buffer that disappears on power failure:

def save_term(node_id, term) do
  tab = table_name(node_id)
  :ok = :dets.insert(tab, {:current_term, term})
  :ok = :dets.sync(tab)
end

An honest caveat: DETS's sync/1 calls Erlang's internal file sync, which should translate to fsync on most platforms — but the documentation doesn't make a hard guarantee. In a production system, you'd want to verify this or use a storage engine that guarantees data actually hits the disk. For this implementation, DETS gave me crash recovery with zero dependencies, and I accepted the tradeoff.

A consensus algorithm that doesn't persist to disk is a distributed system that quietly lies to you.

Who Deserves Your Vote?

A node doesn't vote for the first candidate that asks. Three conditions must all hold:

defp can_grant_vote?(rpc, data) do
  term_ok = rpc.term >= data.current_term
  vote_ok = data.voted_for == nil or data.voted_for == rpc.candidate_id
  log_ok  = log_up_to_date?(rpc.last_log_term, rpc.last_log_index, data.node_id)
  term_ok and vote_ok and log_ok
end

The first two are mechanical. The third is the one that actually makes Raft safe:

defp log_up_to_date?(cand_last_term, cand_last_index, node_id) do
  my_last_term  = Log.last_term(node_id)
  my_last_index = Log.last_index(node_id)

  cond do
    cand_last_term > my_last_term  -> true
    cand_last_term < my_last_term  -> false
    true -> cand_last_index >= my_last_index
  end
end

This is the Election Restriction (§5.4.1). A candidate must have a log at least as up-to-date as the voter's — higher last term wins; if equal, longer log wins.

Here's why this single check carries Raft's entire safety guarantee: a committed entry exists on a majority of servers. An election requires a majority of votes. Those two majorities must overlap by at least one server. That overlapping server will refuse to vote for any candidate whose log doesn't include the committed entry.

The voting mechanics aren't the safety guarantee. The log check is.

The No-Op After Winning

The first thing a new leader does is append an empty entry to its log:

defp become_leader(data) do
  last_index = Log.last_index(data.node_id)
  peers = Cluster.peers(data.cluster, data.node_id)

  next_index  = Map.new(peers, fn p -> {p, last_index + 1} end)
  match_index = Map.new(peers, fn p -> {p, 0} end)

  data = %{data | next_index: next_index, match_index: match_index,
                  leader_id: data.node_id}

  # Append a no-op before taking any client commands.
  {:ok, _} = Log.append(data.node_id, data.current_term, {:noop})

  data = do_send_heartbeats(data)
  {:next_state, :leader, data, [{:timeout, @heartbeat_interval, :heartbeat}]}
end

This confused me the first time I read §5.4.2. Why send a blank entry?

A new leader might inherit log entries from the previous term — entries replicated to a majority but never committed before the old leader crashed. Raft's commit rule says a leader can only commit entries from its own term. It can't reach back and directly commit the previous leader's work.

By committing a no-op in its own term, the new leader pulls everything before it across the commit line at once. Without it, those inherited entries could sit in limbo indefinitely — replicated but never applied, invisible to the state machine.

The no-op isn't ceremony. It's the mechanism that releases the previous leader's unfinished work.

Log Replication: Where I Spent Most of My Debugging Time

Election decides who's in charge. Replication is what actually makes every server identical.

Every 50ms — and immediately after any client command — the leader sends AppendEntries RPCs to each follower. Each RPC carries new entries, but also something more subtle: prev_log_index and prev_log_term, the index and term of the entry immediately before the new ones.

defp send_append_entries_to(data, peer) do
  next_idx       = Map.get(data.next_index, peer, 1)
  prev_log_index = next_idx - 1
  prev_log_term  = prev_log_term(data.node_id, prev_log_index)
  entries        = Log.get_from(data.node_id, next_idx)

  rpc = %AppendEntries{
    term:           data.current_term,
    leader_id:      data.node_id,
    prev_log_index: prev_log_index,
    prev_log_term:  prev_log_term,
    entries:        entries,
    leader_commit:  data.commit_index
  }
  RPC.send_rpc(data.node_id, peer, rpc)
end

Before accepting new entries, a follower checks: "Do I have an entry at prev_log_index with term prev_log_term?" If yes, everything up to that point is consistent and the new entries append cleanly. If no, the logs diverged somewhere, and the follower rejects the request.

This is the Log Matching Property: if two logs agree on the entry at a given index and term, every preceding entry is guaranteed identical too. One check, on every message, maintaining consistency across the entire cluster.

Three Cases When Entries Arrive

def append_entries(node_id, _prev_log_index, entries) do
  Enum.reduce_while(entries, :ok, fn {index, term, command}, _acc ->
    case get(node_id, index) do
      {^index, ^term, _} ->
        # Same index, same term — already have it. Skip.
        {:cont, :ok}

      {^index, _different_term, _} ->
        # Same index, different term — stale entry from a crashed leader.
        # Truncate from here and write the new leader's version.
        truncate_from(node_id, index)
        append_remaining(node_id, [{index, term, command} | entries_from(entries, index + 1)])
        {:halt, :ok}

      nil ->
        # New index — just append.
        append_remaining(node_id, [{index, term, command} | entries_from(entries, index + 1)])
        {:halt, :ok}
    end
  end)
end

The conflict case is the one worth staring at. A previous leader wrote entries to this follower but crashed before committing them. The new leader won the election with different entries at those positions. The follower discards its stale entries and accepts the new leader's version.

This is safe because the discarded entries were never committed — no client was ever told they succeeded. Commitment requires a majority, the majority was never reached, and now the entries disappear. This is exactly what majority-based commitment protects: nothing visible to clients gets erased.

When Is Something Actually Committed?

The leader tracks how far each follower has replicated in match_index. To find the safe commit point, it looks for the highest index where a majority of servers have confirmed:

defp maybe_advance_commit(data) do
  leader_match = Log.last_index(data.node_id)
  all_match    = [leader_match | Map.values(data.match_index)]
  quorum_n     = Cluster.quorum_match_index(all_match)
  entry_term   = Log.term_at(data.node_id, quorum_n)

  if quorum_n > data.commit_index and entry_term == data.current_term do
    data = %{data | commit_index: quorum_n}
    apply_committed_entries(data)
  else
    data
  end
end

quorum_match_index sorts all match indices descending and takes the value at position majority - 1. For three nodes with indices [5, 4, 3], majority is 2, so we take index 1 → value 4. At least 2 of 3 nodes have entries up to index 4.

But there's one more guard — entry_term == data.current_term — and this single line took me longer to truly understand than any other in the codebase.

The failure mode: a leader sees an old entry replicated to a majority and tries to commit it. But that entry was written by a previous leader in a previous term. A different subsequent leader could have legally overwritten it on some nodes after winning an election. Committing based on replication count alone would mean Raft lies about what's durable.

So the current leader commits only entries from its own term. Once it commits a current-term entry, all prior entries get committed along with it. This is why the no-op exists — it's the trigger that releases everything the previous leader left behind.

If you're implementing this, study Figure 8 in the paper until you can reproduce the five-node failure scenario from memory. Then read it once more.

Snapshotting: The Part Everyone Underestimates

The log grows forever unless you compact it. Every 100 entries, my implementation takes a snapshot of the state machine's current state and discards the log entries that produced it.

This sounds simple until you realize it intersects with everything else.

The first problem: a follower falls so far behind that the leader has already compacted the log entries it needs. The leader can't backfill with AppendEntries because those entries no longer exist. Instead, it ships the snapshot in 64KB chunks via InstallSnapshot RPCs. The logic lives inside the same path that sends AppendEntries — when the leader sees that a peer's nextIndex is behind the snapshot's last included index, it sends a chunk, waits for the follower's acknowledgment, then sends the next chunk:

defp send_append_entries_to(data, peer) do
  next_idx = Map.get(data.next_index, peer, 1)
  snap = Snapshot.load(data.node_id)

  if snap != nil and next_idx <= snap.last_included_index do
    # Follower needs entries we've discarded — send snapshot in chunks
    offset = Map.get(data.snapshot_progress, peer, 0)
    remaining = max(0, byte_size(snap.data) - offset)
    chunk_len = min(@snapshot_chunk_bytes, remaining)
    chunk = binary_part(snap.data, offset, chunk_len)
    done = offset + byte_size(chunk) >= byte_size(snap.data)

    rpc = %InstallSnapshot{
      term: data.current_term,
      leader_id: data.node_id,
      last_included_index: snap.last_included_index,
      last_included_term: snap.last_included_term,
      offset: offset, data: chunk, done: done
    }
    RPC.send_rpc(data.node_id, peer, rpc)
  else
    # Normal path: send AppendEntries with log entries
    # ...
  end
end

It's request-response: send one chunk, wait for InstallSnapshotReply with the acknowledged offset, then send the next. The follower's reply drives the progress; the leader doesn't blast all chunks at once.

The second problem is more subtle: when does the snapshot happen relative to in-flight appends? If the leader is sending entries 101–110 to a follower and simultaneously compacts entries 1–100 into a snapshot, the next AppendEntries needs prev_log_index 100 — but that entry is now inside the snapshot. The leader has to detect this case and fall back to sending the snapshot instead of trying to reference compacted entries.

The third problem: a follower receiving a snapshot must replace its state machine, truncate its log up to the snapshot's last included index, and update its commit index — all as a single operation. If it crashes halfway through (say, after replacing the state machine but before truncating the log), it wakes up in a broken state where the log and state machine disagree. The snapshot effectively becomes the new "beginning of time" for that node's log.

None of this is new or clever. But every interaction between snapshotting and the rest of the system is a place where a crash at the wrong moment can corrupt state. The paper covers it in about two pages. The implementation demanded more paranoia than any other feature.

Membership Changes: Where the Paper Undersells the Danger

Adding a node to a running cluster sounds like a configuration update. It isn't. This was the section of the implementation where I had to slow down and think hardest about why the simple approach is dangerously wrong before the Raft solution made any sense.

Why You Can't Just Switch Configs

Say the cluster is {A, B, C} and you want to add D and E, making it {A, B, C, D, E}. The obvious approach: send every server a message saying "the cluster is now five nodes." But messages don't arrive simultaneously. There's always a window where some servers are on the old config and others are on the new one.

Imagine this moment in time: C, D, and E have received the update and switched to the new config. A and B haven't yet — they still think the cluster is {A, B, C}. Now a network partition splits the cluster into {A, B} on one side and {C, D, E} on the other.

A and B are using the old config where the cluster has 3 members. They are 2 of 3 — that's a majority. They elect A as leader and start accepting writes.

C, D, and E are using the new config where the cluster has 5 members. They are 3 of 5 — also a majority. They elect C as leader and start accepting writes.

Two leaders, both legitimate under their own view of the cluster, both acknowledging client commands. When the partition heals, the cluster has two divergent histories. The root cause isn't the partition — Raft handles partitions fine normally. The problem is that two different definitions of "majority" existed at the same time, and they could be satisfied by non-overlapping groups of servers.

Joint Consensus: Requiring Both Sides to Agree

Raft prevents this by never jumping directly from old config to new config. Instead, it introduces a transitional state where every decision — elections, commits, everything — must be approved by a majority of both the old group and the new group simultaneously.

The leader drives this by appending a special log entry (the "joint config") that activates dual-majority mode. Once committed, the rule becomes: you need at least 2 of {A, B, C} AND at least 3 of {A, B, C, D, E} to do anything.

Now replay that same partition. {A, B} on one side, {C, D, E} on the other:

A and B — 2 of 3 in the old group (passes), but only 2 of 5 in the new group (need 3 — fails). Can't elect a leader.

C, D, E — only 1 of 3 in the old group (need 2 — fails). Can't elect a leader either.

Neither side can act independently. Any group large enough to satisfy both majority requirements must overlap with any other such group — so two independent leaders can never happen.

def has_joint_majority?(votes_received, current_cluster, joint_config) do
  case joint_config do
    nil ->
      has_majority?(MapSet.size(votes_received), length(current_cluster))

    {old_cluster, new_cluster} ->
      old_votes = Enum.count(old_cluster, &MapSet.member?(votes_received, &1))
      new_votes = Enum.count(new_cluster, &MapSet.member?(votes_received, &1))
      has_majority?(old_votes, length(old_cluster)) and
        has_majority?(new_votes, length(new_cluster))
  end
end

Once the cluster is stable in dual-majority mode, the leader appends a second log entry — the finalization — that says "transition complete, the cluster is now {A, B, C, D, E}, old config no longer matters." This commits under dual-majority rules (both groups agree), and then everyone switches to the new config. Two entries, not one, because the first is the safety net and the second is the cleanup.

The Edge Cases That Surprised Me

The paper describes joint consensus cleanly. The implementation surfaced questions the paper doesn't linger on.

What happens when the leader crashes mid-transition — after committing the joint config entry but before the finalization? The new leader gets elected under dual-majority rules (because the joint entry is committed and active). It can't restart the transition from scratch because the joint entry is already committed — that's a fact the cluster agreed on. It has to continue where the old leader left off by appending and committing the finalization. There's no special resume logic: the new leader simply replicates whatever is in its log. If the old leader appended the finalization entry before crashing, it's already there; the new leader just sends it to followers until it commits. Normal replication handles it.

What happens when the leader removes itself? Say the cluster goes from {A, B, C} to {B, C, D}. A drives the entire transition, commits the finalization, and then realizes it's not in the new config. It schedules a delayed shutdown:

if acc.node_id not in new_cluster do
  Process.send_after(self(), :shutdown_not_in_cluster, 100)
end

The 100ms delay matters — A must stay alive long enough for B and C to receive the commit acknowledgment and start an election among {B, C, D}. Immediate shutdown orphans the commit.

And the interaction that was hardest to debug: new nodes D and E join with empty logs and need to catch up, potentially via snapshot transfer if the leader has compacted its log. But the finalization entry needs a majority of the new config to commit — which might depend on D and E having caught up enough to confirm replication. So the leader is juggling snapshot transfers to new nodes and a membership transition simultaneously, and both can fail independently. That concurrency was where most of my debugging time went.

Two Optimizations That Aren't Optional

Pre-Vote: Protecting a Healthy Cluster from a Recovering Node

A node gets partitioned from the cluster. It can't reach the leader, so its election timer fires repeatedly. It starts elections, increments its term, gets no quorum, increments again. The rest of the cluster runs fine. The partitioned node climbs to term 847 while the cluster is on term 12.

When the partition heals, this node rejoins. Every server sees the higher term, accepts it, and forces the healthy leader to step down. The cluster was working perfectly, and a recovering node just disrupted it for no reason.

Pre-vote prevents this. Before incrementing its term, the node sends lightweight PreVote probes: "If I started an election, would you vote for me?" No state changes — no term increment, no vote record, nothing persisted.

The rejection mechanism is what makes it work: when a PreVote arrives, the follower checks whether the candidate's log is at least as up-to-date as its own. A partitioned node has a stale log — it hasn't seen the commits the cluster made while it was disconnected. So the log check fails, the pre-vote is denied, and the partitioned node never inflates its term. The healthy majority rejects it; it concludes the cluster is fine and holds steady:

defp start_pre_vote(data, _from_role) do
  rpc = %PreVote{
    term: data.current_term + 1,  # hypothetical — not yet incremented
    candidate_id: data.node_id,
    last_log_index: Log.last_index(data.node_id),
    last_log_term:  Log.last_term(data.node_id)
  }

  for peer <- Cluster.peers(data.cluster, data.node_id) do
    RPC.send_rpc(data.node_id, peer, rpc)
  end
end

Pre-vote isn't in the original paper — it's from the extended dissertation. But without it, every network partition ends with an unnecessary re-election when the partition heals. In any real deployment, this is the first extension worth adding.

Fast Log Backtracking

When a follower rejects AppendEntries because its log diverged, the leader needs to find where the logs split. The naive approach: decrement nextIndex by 1 and retry. For a follower that's 1,000 entries behind, that's 1,000 round trips.

Instead, the follower includes conflict information in its rejection — the term of the conflicting entry and the first index with that term. The leader jumps directly to the right position:

defp find_conflict(node_id, prev_log_index) do
  case Log.get(node_id, prev_log_index) do
    nil ->
      {max(1, Log.last_index(node_id)), nil}
    {_, term, _} ->
      first = Log.first_index_for_term(node_id, term) || prev_log_index
      {first, term}
  end
end

Log divergences almost always span a single term — one leader wrote a batch before crashing. This reduces catch-up from O(entries) to O(distinct terms), which in practice means one or two round trips instead of hundreds.

Architecture

RaftEx.Supervisor (one_for_one)
├── TcpConnectionPool     — outbound TCP, one connection per peer
├── Inspector             — telemetry event handler
└── NodeSupervisor        — DynamicSupervisor
    └── Server (per node) — gen_statem FSM
        └── TcpListener   — inbound TCP acceptor

Nodes communicate over TCP with 4-byte length-prefixed binary framing, serialized with Erlang's native term format. The connection pool handles reconnection transparently — if a send fails, it drops the socket, opens a new one, retries once. Transport failures never surface to the Raft logic.

Each node gets its own DETS files for metadata, log entries, and snapshot data. The telemetry layer logs every state transition, RPC, commit, and apply as it happens:

[NODE :n1] [LEADER] 📝 appended index=4 term=2 cmd={:set, "key", "val"}
[NODE :n1] → SEND AppendEntries to n2
[NODE :n2] ← REPLY AppendEntriesReply to n1
[NODE :n1] ✔ COMMITTED up to index=4
[NODE :n1] ⚙ APPLIED index=4 result={:ok, "val"}

I ran the fault tolerance tests with this output visible — killed the leader mid-write, watched the cluster elect a new one in under 300ms, catch up the lagging follower, and resume without losing a single entry. The sequence below is a simplified view of what the telemetry shows:

[NODE :n1] [LEADER] 📝 appended index=7 term=3 cmd={:set, "x", "42"}
[NODE :n1] → SEND AppendEntries to n2, n3
  ── n1 crashes ──
[NODE :n2] [CANDIDATE] ⏰ election timeout → starting election term=4
[NODE :n3] [FOLLOWER] ✅ granted vote to n2 for term=4
[NODE :n2] [LEADER] 🏆 WON election! term=4, votes=2
[NODE :n2] [LEADER] 📝 appended index=8 term=4 cmd=:noop
[NODE :n2] ✔ COMMITTED up to index=8

Seven committed entries survive the leader's death. The no-op at index 8 pulls the previous leader's work across the commit line. No data lost.

What I'd Tell Someone Building This

The Raft paper is 18 pages. My implementation is 1,200 lines. That ratio shows how well the paper is written — most mechanisms translate directly into functions.

One practice saved me repeatedly: annotating every function with the paper section it implements. Comments like # §5.3 Rule 2 and # §5.4.2 throughout. Weeks later, when a test failed, I could trace the bug to the exact paragraph in the paper.

The algorithm itself isn't complex. The correctness depends on discipline — the orderings you enforce, the persistence you don't skip, the edge cases you don't dismiss as unlikely. Every shortcut I considered turned out to be a bug I hadn't written yet.

The full source, with a test suite and every rule cited back to the paper, is at github.com/ProgMastermind/raft-elixir.
